✔️ Can increase risk of seizures

Addison’s → low aldosterone → lose sodium + water → hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia
Cushing’s → high (or cortisol acting like it) → retain sodium + water → hypertension, hypokalemia. Watch the full episode in our bio!

SNRIs: know how they’re different from SSRIs 💊🧠
SNRI = Serotonin + Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor
👉 Increases BOTH serotonin and norepinephrine
High-yield NCLEX points:
✔️ Used for depression & anxiety
✔️ Can also help with neuropathic pain
✔️ May increase blood pressure
✔️

MAOI = Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitor
👉 Increases serotonin, norepinephrine & dopamine
Remember:
✔️ Used when other antidepressants fail
✔️ STRICT tyramine restriction (aged cheese, cured meats, red wine)
✔️ Risk for hypertensive crisis
✔️ High risk for serotonin syndrome
✔️ 2-week

🚗 Red blood cells are the Uber drivers of your body.
Their job? Deliver oxygen to every tissue and organ that needs it.
When anemia hits, those drivers start disappearing—and oxygen delivery slows down. The result? Fatigue, weakness, shortness of breath, and tissues that aren’t

Thrombocytopenia is a condition characterized by a low platelet count in the 🩸 blood, which can lead to increased ⬆️ risk of bleeding.

Platelets are used to form clots...so with a low platelet count, clot formation will be 🚫 impaired!

Low platelet levels ⬇️ can be caused by v

Choosing unstable before stable is a core NCLEX® prioritization strategy.
Stable clients have unchanged conditions with expected findings and consistent trends in vital signs and labs.
Unstable clients show change, new or unexpected findings, abnormal critical labs, or worsening

The 4️⃣ methods to use when assessing your clients are... 👇

👀 Inspection: visual assessment of the client
✋ Palpation: use of touch to assess the skin and underlying tissues
〰️ Percussion: tapping the skin with fingertips to produce vibrations over tissues and organs

This infection can cause localized inflammation and pain as well as systemic symptoms.
Pyelonephritis can be classified as either acute or chronic:

When the NCLEX® asks you to identify which client should be your priority, a useful trick is to choose the most unstable client before the more stable ones. Take them one at a time and figure out who needs your help first - and who can wait.

Treatment for glomerulonephritis will depend on the cause of the inflammation. 🧐
Most commonly, this is a strep infection - so as nurses, we need to be educating our clients about the importance of completing the entire course of antibiotics! 💊
We also want to monitor I/Os 💧 f

Kidney stones (also known as renal calculi or nephrolithiasis) are made up of solid mineral deposits, most commonly calcium oxalate, that form in the kidneys.
Anyone can get a kidney stone, but we see an increased prevalence in males, those with a family history of kidney stones,

Let's take a closer look at some of the sounds you might hear when auscultating your clients' lungs! 👇

🫁 Rales (crackles): there are two types...fine and course - fine crackles are like short, high-pitched pops, while coarse crackles are lower-pitched and last longer

🫁 Rhonc

▪️ Hypertension
▪️ Flank/abdominal pain
▪️ Constipation
▪️ Hematuria, nocturia, & UTIs
▪️ Kidney stones
▪️ Increased abdominal girth

Shelley’s nursing journey began right after getting married—and like many paths in nursing, it wasn’t linear.
After working in pediatrics, she stepped away to raise her four kids. Coming back? Intimidating. Full of doubt. Wondering if she still had what it takes.
But step by step

Nursing isn’t a perfect journey.
There are moments you question yourself. Moments you feel like you’re not cut out for it.
But those moments? They’re part of becoming the nurse you’re meant to be.
We see it every day—and we’re here for it.
Keep going. You’ve got this. 💫

Nurse Anatomy Quiz for Nursing School Students 

What is the largest vein in the human body? 
a. superior vena cava
b. inferior vena cava 
c. femoral vein
d. pulmonary vein

Answer is B: Inferior vena cava. This is the body's largest vein. It takes deoxygenated blood from the low

Nurse Routes to Take a Temperature (Most Common):

-Oral (mouth) = most common route; place under tongue (posterior sublingual pocket)

-Tympanic (ear) = place in ear per device instructions using provided cap/sheath

-Axillary (in armpit) = often 0.5 to 1°F LOWER than oral route
